top_gaps: Does not propose concrete mitigation strategies or analyze conditions under which collapse can be avoided.
Focused on image diffusion models; generalization to language models and broader data types remains unexplored.
Theoretical guarantees are limited to specific model families and may not extend to large-scale LLMs.
Does not quantify the rate of AI content growth on the web or propose large-scale mitigation strategies.
Curation process itself assumed ideal; does not address practical cost or scalability of human-preference filtering.
Evaluated primarily on image generation; applicability to language model self-training loops is not demonstrated.
Surplexity as a metric needs validation at scale and its robustness to adversarial synthetic content is untested.
Does not provide theoretical bounds on collapse rate or propose concrete mitigation strategies for LLMs.
Theory may not fully capture the complexity of modern large-scale models trained on diverse, mixed real-synthetic data.
Analysis is conducted in simplified statistical settings that may not fully reflect the dynamics of modern transformer-based LLMs.
Does not propose concrete architectural solutions or quantify the rate of retrieval collapse across different search systems.
Focuses on a narrow educational domain and does not generalize findings to broader web or open-access content ecosystems.
Does not address mitigation strategies or quantify collapse rates empirically at scale.
Does not quantify the scale of AI-generated wildlife content online or empirically measure impact on conservation attitudes.
Detection methodology may not generalize across languages or capture subtle stylistic AI traces without extensive labeled data.
Runet-specific findings may not transfer to other language or media ecosystems, and ground-truth labeling remains methodologically challenging.
Relies largely on theoretical and qualitative analysis without rigorous empirical measurement of bot or AI content fractions.
Focuses on images rather than text and on a single election event, limiting generalizability to ongoing AI content pollution dynamics.
Addresses image data rather than text, and does not quantify long-term model degradation from cumulative synthetic contamination.
Focuses on user perception of labels rather than actual prevalence or quality of AI content, and does not address training data implications.
Focuses on image models; does not address watermarking robustness against adversarial removal or cross-modal training contamination.
Focuses narrowly on a shared task dataset; does not assess real-world prevalence of AI reviews in Dravidian-language platforms.
Does not address robustness of the watermark to adversarial paraphrasing or removal attacks.
Retrieval defense requires access to the original AI-generated text corpus, limiting applicability in open-web settings.
Perplexity-based detection may degrade as students use prompt engineering or paraphrasing to lower perplexity of AI-generated code.
Does not quantify detection failure rates under adversarial conditions or address feedback loops with generative model improvement.
Does not address how detection performance degrades as generative models are iteratively improved on human feedback.
Does not assess robustness of watermarking schemes against adversarial removal or model-collapse-induced content shifts.
Does not examine how evolving generative models systematically degrade detector reliability over time.
Lacks empirical evaluation of detector performance and does not address the long-term scalability of detection approaches.
Does not examine how diversity decline affects downstream task performance or how it interacts with data curation strategies.
Does not connect lexical diversity metrics to AI-generated text quality or model training implications.
Does not address how these diversity metrics shift when AI-generated text is mixed into corpora.
Does not examine how diversity metrics degrade under recursive or synthetic data training.
Does not address implications for recursive training or synthetic data pollution scenarios.
Does not address application of complexity metrics to AI-generated or synthetic texts.
Does not connect L2 lexical diversity patterns to AI-generated text detection or model training data quality.
Does not address implications for large-scale web corpora where translationese and AI-generated text co-occur.
Does not empirically apply the framework to recursive or iteratively trained generative models to assess collapse-related evaluation failures.
Does not study downstream effects on model collapse when distilled models are used as teachers in subsequent rounds.
Does not fully address long-term recursive feedback loops where models are retrained on their own synthetic outputs across generations.
Does not study how AI-generated synthetic data in the corpus affects model collapse over iterative training cycles.
Does not address temporal degradation of web data quality or AI content contamination in low-resource corpora.
Does not systematically analyze the effect of AI-generated data contamination in the pretraining corpus.
Does not examine how CQF interacts with AI-generated content contamination or model collapse dynamics.
Does not study recursive training on synthetic data or potential model collapse in multilingual settings.
Does not address how AI-generated content fraction in the data affects proxy model reliability.
Does not measure temporal trends in web content quality or AI-generated text prevalence in crawled data.
Does not consider the effect of AI-generated synthetic data on quality-diversity trade-offs over iterative training.
Does not examine how AI-generated content proportion affects sample-level quality-diversity balance over time.
Does not systematically study whether recursive AI-feedback loops degrade model quality over multiple generations.
Does not quantify how much human data is truly irreplaceable for safety-critical alignment properties.
Does not compare annotation cost-effectiveness with AI-generated preference labels.
Does not address the quality and cost of the underlying human preference labels used for ranking.
Does not evaluate whether self-generated reward signals degrade when training data is predominantly AI-generated.
Does not address the scalability crisis as human annotation becomes increasingly expensive relative to AI-generated alternatives.
Assumes high-quality human preference data is available and does not address the cost or scalability of collecting such data.
Does not examine whether AI feedback quality degrades as the AI models themselves are trained on increasing amounts of AI-generated data.
Does not quantify the marginal cost of human annotation versus synthetic feedback at scale.
Does not propose a concrete fix to eliminate sycophancy without sacrificing alignment quality.
Does not address governance, provenance, or ethical frameworks for the human data collected.
Does not address data provenance, consent management, or long-term data governance for the collected behavioral signals.
Does not evaluate the framework on large-scale real-world AI training workflows or address adversarial manipulation of off-chain components.
Focuses on genomics/biomedical domain and does not translate findings to general-purpose AI training data governance.
Does not prescribe concrete technical standards or platforms to remedy the identified governance deficiencies.
Remains conceptual and does not provide a concrete platform or technical solution for redistributing data control to contributors.
Focuses on biomedical meta-analysis and does not extend the harmonization framework to AI training data ecosystems.
Scoped to mental health measurement tools and does not generalize the version-control governance model to broader AI training data provenance.
Domain-specific to archaeology and Indigenous heritage; does not address how these governance principles could scale to AI training data curation.
Narrowly focused on UK clinical trial regulation and does not address broader AI training data provenance or governance frameworks.
Does not measure longitudinal quality drift of web data or the increasing presence of AI-generated content in future crawls.
Does not track how increasing AI-generated content in future crawls may degrade dataset quality over time.
Focuses on a single snapshot rather than longitudinal changes in web corpus quality over time.
Relies on imperfect detectors and does not quantify downstream effects of AI-contaminated Wikipedia on LLM retraining.
Focuses on legal document text rather than general web content quality, leaving open questions about broader temporal shifts in web corpus characteristics.
Does not examine text quality or content composition of the web, leaving open how platform consolidation affects the linguistic diversity of training data.
Focuses on event extraction accuracy rather than measuring longitudinal shifts in Wikipedia content quality or AI-generated text prevalence.
Does not connect privacy policy evolution to AI training data governance or measure effects on web-corpus usability.
Focuses on structural/technical web evolution rather than text quality or AI training data implications.
Temporal analysis is limited to a single health crisis and does not generalize to broader web-text quality trends relevant to AI training corpora.
Focuses on text-based, English social reasoning without cross-cultural or multimodal evaluation.
Does not propose training interventions or data curation strategies to improve social reasoning in LLMs.
Does not investigate training data composition or fine-tuning strategies that might improve ToM performance.
Does not systematically study the role of training data quality or human annotation in achieving genuine social reasoning.
Embodied setting limits applicability to purely text-based LLM social reasoning evaluation.
Does not address how culturally diverse training data or curation strategies could improve cross-cultural ToM performance.
Does not address how such systems scale or generalize across diverse cultural contexts for social reasoning.
Focuses on nonverbal cues but leaves open how to integrate verbal and nonverbal signals jointly for richer social intelligence evaluation.
Leaves open how to handle very large group interactions and more complex social dynamics beyond speaker-level attention alignment.
Does not propose concrete remedies for the identified spurious correlations or how to build more robust social reasoning benchmarks.
Does not analyze the long-term quality degradation when iteratively training on self-generated instructions, which connects to model collapse concerns.
Does not investigate the scalability ceiling of human annotation or how quality degrades as community-contributed data grows.
Does not address how to systematically identify or generate high-quality curation criteria at scale, nor the effect on downstream social reasoning tasks.
Does not evaluate whether increasingly complex synthetic instructions introduce distributional artifacts or hasten model collapse in iterative training.
Does not address how self-guided selection scales to continual or domain-specific fine-tuning regimes.
Does not investigate how in-context alignment interacts with data composition or quality of demonstrations at scale.
Does not examine cost-effectiveness of human post-editing at large scale or for non-dialogical tasks.
Does not examine cost-effectiveness of human post-editing at large scale or for non-dialogical tasks.
Does not systematically ablate quality vs. quantity trade-offs within the dataset or assess long-term model collapse from synthetic data.
Does not address how grounding in reference documents scales across diverse domains or languages.
Does not address model collapse, data pollution, or any aspect of the thesis taxonomy.
Entirely outside the scope of LLM training data quality, web content, or model collapse.
Does not connect personality dissociation to training data composition or alignment procedures.
Qualitative and interpretive approach leaves empirical measurement of AI content growth or quality change unaddressed.
